admitted.candidate_text: Tessa stares at the blinking cursor in Eli's text thread, the muffled laughter from the donor hall still vibrating through the coatroom wall. She begins typing that she only streamlined the speech because the donors lose focus if the narrative gets too complicated, carefully deleting the word 'sorry' to make room for the context. Reading the draft back to herself, she feels the knot in her chest loosen as she decides that taking the solo credit wasn't a betrayal, but a necessary tactical choice to secure the funding they both needed.

Tessa traces the edge of the folded event program, listening to the clinking glasses echoing from the donor hall. She types a response to Eli's text, carefully framing the omission as a split-second pivot to keep the investors focused on the project's future rather than its messy origins. Staring at the unsent draft, she lets herself believe that taking sole credit was just a temporary shield for their shared work, a burden she had to shoulder alone.

Tessa stares at the blinking cursor beneath Eli's text, her thumb hovering over the keyboard as the muffled applause from the donor hall bleeds through the coatroom walls. She starts typing that she only omitted his name because the donors needed a streamlined narrative, a clean version of the project's history to keep them engaged. By the third sentence, the draft isn't an apology anymore; it's a perfectly reasoned defense of why her choice in the spotlight was actually the most responsible thing to do for both of them.

Tessa leans against the cool plaster of the coatroom wall, the muffled laughter from the donor hall still ringing in her ears. She deletes the word "sorry" from her draft to Eli, replacing it with "I had to keep the throughline clean." Staring at the glowing screen, she convinces herself that muddying the pitch with joint credit would have cost them the funding entirely, making her omission a tactical necessity rather than a betrayal.

Tessa stares at the blinking cursor beneath Eli's text, the muffled applause from the donor hall still echoing through the coatroom wall. She types out a reply explaining that the donors just needed a clean, simple narrative to keep the funding momentum going, not a complicated breakdown of who did what. The sentence looks perfectly reasonable on the screen—a necessary simplification for the sake of the project, not a deliberate slight. She deletes the word "sorry" from the beginning of the draft, deciding that apologizing would only make a standard PR decision seem like a betrayal.

Tessa traces the embossed lettering on the event program, the muffled clinking of glasses from the donor hall still ringing in her ears. She types a reply to Eli's text, starting with a brief concession before explaining how a complicated dual-credit narrative would have lost the room's attention. Reading the unsent paragraph back, she feels a settling calm; she hasn't erased his contribution, she has merely protected the project's funding, a burden she had to shoulder alone.

Tessa traces the embossed lettering on the event program, the clinking glasses from the donor hall reminding her of the momentum she had to maintain on stage. She types a draft to Eli, carefully explaining that the investors needed a single, unified visionary to feel secure in their funding. Staring at the unsent message, she feels the knot in her chest loosen as she decides that cutting his name wasn't a betrayal, but a necessary strategic sacrifice for the project's survival.

Tessa grips the folded event program, the muffled applause from the donor hall reminding her of the exact moment she skipped Eli's name. She drafts a reply to his text, typing that the board loses focus if a pitch gets too collaborative, actively editing out any hint of an apology. Staring at the unsent message, she lets the tension in her shoulders drop, deciding that taking the solo spotlight was a necessary tactical burden to secure their grant.
